content.assessment: This proposal leaves the selection, tracking, and admissions mechanisms entirely unchanged and instead attempts to alter incentives through funding conditionality. Institutionally, it does not touch the centralized admissions process, the early tracking ages, or the church/private exemption structure; its effectiveness depends on maintainers' behavioral response to funding incentives, which is outside the scope of the institutional description available here.
opportunities: Could be layered onto the existing centralized testing regime without requiring new legal admissions criteria, reducing institutional friction relative to structural reform
strengths: Uses existing testing infrastructure (ABC testing at grades 6, 8, 10) rather than requiring new institutional machinery, which may ease administrative rollout
threats: Church and private schools' exemption from standard state admissions procedures could allow the same maintainers most associated with segregation precedent to route around funding-based incentives entirely
weaknesses: Does not alter the centralized admissions process, the age-based entry points, or the differentiated governance status of church/private maintainers that currently sit outside standard rules
No institutional mechanism is proposed to prevent maintainers from adjusting non-SES criteria to preserve current selection patterns while nominally complying with funding conditions
